news_summary: Today's technology news highlights breakthroughs in neuroscience with a complete fruit fly neural map, innovative theories on life's origins, advancements in research integrity through a journal-flagging tool, and enhanced efficiency in blue perovskite LED development.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité technologique d'aujourd'hui met en avant des avancées majeures en neurosciences, avec la cartographie complète du système nerveux de la mouche du vinaigre, des théories novatrices sur les origines de la vie, des progrès en matière d'intégrité de la recherche grâce à un outil de signalement des revues, ainsi qu'une efficacité accrue dans le développement des LED à pérovskite bleue.
news_summary_es: Las noticias tecnológicas de hoy destacan los avances en neurociencia con un mapa neuronal completo de la mosca de la fruta, teorías innovadoras sobre los orígenes de la vida, progresos en la integridad de la investigación gracias a una herramienta de señalización de revistas científicas y una mayor eficiencia en el desarrollo de LED de perovskita azul.

articles.title: Scientists mapped every neural connection in a fruit fly and found a surprise
articles.summary: A groundbreaking new connectome maps every neural connection in an adult fruit fly’s central nervous system, creating an unprecedented view of how the brain and body work together. The findings suggest that complex behaviors emerge from distributed local circuits rather than a single central controller, offering new clues about intelligence, movement, and brain function.
articles.content:
A large international research team led by groups at Harvard Medical School and Princeton University has reached a major neuroscience milestone by publishing a complete map of every connection between neurons in the central nervous system of an adult fruit fly.

The achievement gives scientists a new way to examine how the brain and body work together to produce complex actions, including walking and flying. It also opens the door to broader studies of the core rules that govern nervous systems.

"We can see all of the neurons and their connections as a complete unit for the first time and ask, 'What do we learn from that?'" said study co-senior author Rachel Wilson, the Joseph B. Martin Professor of Basic Research in the Field of Neurobiology in the Blavatnik Institute at HMS.

First Complete Fruit Fly Brain and Body Wiring Map

The new map of neural connections, known as a connectome, extends a previously published fruit fly brain connectome by adding the fly's spinal cord equivalent, called the nerve cord.

"It is really important to have a central nervous system connectome that is as complete as possible so we can link up the brain and body and start thinking about behavior holistically," said study co-senior author Wei-Chung Allen Lee, associate professor of neurobiology at HMS and HMS professor of neurology at Boston Children's Hospital.

When the team studied the connectome, they discovered that many fruit fly behaviors appear to be directed by local neural circuits in the relevant body parts, rather than by one central command area in the brain.

The full connectome is now freely available online, giving researchers around the world a powerful new resource for neuroscience studies. The work, published June 8 in Nature, received support in part from U.S. federal funding, including the BRAIN Initiative (Brain Research Through Advancing Innovative Neurotechnologies), National Institutes of Health, and National Science Foundation.

Why Fruit Flies Matter in Neuroscience

One of neuroscience's major unanswered questions is how neurons in the brain and body connect and coordinate to generate behavior. The fruit fly Drosophila melanogaster is a valuable model for exploring that problem.

Fruit flies are simple to breed and keep in the lab. Although their nervous system contains only about 160,000 neurons, they can still perform complex behaviors such as navigating, interacting socially, learning, and reacting to sensory signals. They also have what Lee describes as an incredibly sophisticated genetic toolkit, which allows scientists to access, control, and record activity from single neurons or groups of neurons.

In 2024, the FlyWire Consortium, led by Mala Murthy and Sebastian Seung at Princeton, who are also co-authors of the new study, published a complete connectome of a fruit fly brain. At the same time, Lee and his colleagues were building a connectome of the fruit fly nerve cord, which controls the legs, wings, and other appendages while also processing sensory information.

"The brain and nerve cord connectomes are each useful on their own, but until you can bridge the two, it's hard to understand how information moves between the brain and the body," said co-first author Helen Yang, a research fellow in neurobiology in the Wilson Lab.

Co-first author Alexander Bates, also a research fellow in neurobiology in the Wilson Lab, noted that the brain holds most of the neurons, but the nerve cord contains neurons that are "some of the most useful" because they are tied to sensation, movement, and functions that are often easier to interpret.

Connecting the Brain to the Nerve Cord

The FlyWire team was eager to shift toward the brain and neural cord, or BANC, dataset imaged in the Lee Lab, said co-senior author Murthy, the Karol and Marnie Marcin '96 Professor of Neuroscience at Princeton and director of the Princeton Neuroscience Institute (PNI).

"The new connectome represents a major advance for the field, with the ability to understand how circuits in the brain receive feedback from and control the actions of the body," she said.

"For the first time, we can follow information flow from sensation to action across an entire nervous system," added co-author Arie Matsliah of the PNI.

How Scientists Built the Connectome

To create the connectome, researchers sliced a single fruit fly into thousands of extremely thin serial sections. They then used electron microscopy to capture millions of images showing neurons and their connections. AI tools helped align those images and assemble them into a unified 3D map.

The finished connectome shows how each neuron connects with other neurons in the brain and nerve cord at the level of individual synapses. The map does not cover the fly's entire body, but the researchers used identifiable neurons and previous scientific literature to link central nervous system neurons with neurons in many appendages and sensory organs, effectively "embodying" the connectome.

Lee said scientists can use the connectome to develop new hypotheses for lab experiments. He compares it to having detailed Google Maps information while planning a route.

"The connectome has shown us that most of our hypotheses are too simple. Now, we can develop more complex hypotheses and move forward with experiments to test them," Lee said.

A Surprise About How Movement Is Controlled

The researchers have already used the connectome to study motor control, especially how a fly moves its legs and other body parts.

A long-standing idea in neuroscience holds that the brain acts as a centralized controller that decides which actions an animal will perform. The fruit fly connectome pointed to a different answer.

The team found that motor control in fruit flies mostly occurs locally. For instance, movement of one leg is mainly governed by the neural circuits for that leg. Those circuits then communicate with circuits for the other legs to produce coordinated actions such as walking.

The same pattern appeared in circuits linked to the fly's wings, mouth, and other body parts. The researchers also found that motor circuits connect with other circuit types, including those in the visual and endocrine systems, which supply extra information that helps shape behavior.

"Our findings suggest that control for actions is highly distributed in local modules that link up and work together in different ways," Bates said.

What Comes Next for Connectome Research

The researchers say the connectome could support many future lines of investigation. Yang compares it to the Human Genome Project, another large-scale open resource that has been used in many different ways.

Soon, the team plans to add more information to the connectome, including details about neuropeptides, the small, protein-like molecules that neurons use to communicate.

The connectome may also reveal basic principles that apply to nervous systems across species, including humans. Bates said many discoveries from fruit fly neuroscience have carried over from invertebrates to mammals, including findings related to navigation, olfaction, and memory.

Another goal is "to bring full-connectome mapping to much more complex organisms," said Matsliah. He noted that progress in AI, computing, and open collaborative science is making this kind of research increasingly possible.

A major question now is whether the distributed neural control observed in fruit flies is also found in other animals. Lee is currently investigating that possibility in mice.

"I would be shocked if this is unique to the fly," Yang said. "We don't have this level of resolution in other animals, but we know that they have a lot of these local circuits."

Lessons for Artificial Intelligence

The work could also have implications for artificial intelligence. The connectome offers real biological data that may help guide the design of artificial agents that move through virtual worlds, systems that are increasingly used to study intelligence and improve AI training.

"One thing that always amazes me is that this tiny little fly does a hell of a lot; even our best AI agents and robots can't do everything that a fly does," Yang said. "There may be lessons for AI in how the nervous system is organized."

Authorship, funding, disclosures

Jasper S. Phelps and Minsu Kim are also co-first authors of the study. Jan Drugowitsch is co-senior author. Additional authors include Zaki Ajabi, Eric Perlman, Kevin M. Delgado, Mohammed Abdal Monium Osman, Christopher K. Salmon, Jay Gager, Benjamin Silverman, Sophia Renauld, Farzaan Salman, Janki Patel, Matthew F. Collie, Jingxuan Fan, Diego A. Pacheco, Yunzhi Zhao, Wenyi Zhang, Laia Serratosa Capdevila, Ruairí J.V. Roberts, Eva J. Munnelly, Nina Griggs, Helen Langley, Borja Moya-Llamas, Zuoyu Zhang, Ryan T. Maloney, Szi-chieh Yu, Amy R. Sterling, Marissa Sorek, Krzysztof Kruk, Nikitas Serafetinidis, Serene Dhawan, Finja Klemm, Paul Brooks, Ellen Lesser, Jessica M. Jones, Sara E. Pierce-Lundgren, Su-Yee Lee, Yichen Luo, Andrew P. Cook, Theresa H. McKim, Dimitrios Stasi Giakoumas, Benjamin Gorko, Emily C. Kophs, Tjalda Falt, Alexa M. Negron-Morales, Austin Burke, James Hebditch, Kyle P. Willie, Ryan Willie, Sergiy Popovych, Nico Kemnitz, Dodam Ih, Kisuk Lee, Ran Lu, Akhilesh Halageri, J. Alexander Bae, Ben Jourdan, Gregory Schwartzman, Damian D. Demarest, Emily Behnke, Doug Bland, Anne Kristiansen, Jaime Skelton, Tom Stocks, Dustin Garner, Anthony Hernandez, Sandeep Kumar, The BANC-FlyWire Consortium, Kevin C. Daly, Sven Dorkenwald, Forrest Collman, Marie P. Suver, Lisa M. Fenk, Michael J. Pankratz, Zepeng Yao, Stephen J. Huston, Tomke Stürner, Gregory S.X.E. Jefferis, Katharina Eichler, Andrew M. Seeds, Stefanie Hampel, Sweta Agrawal, Tatsuo S. Okubo, Meet Zandawala, Thomas Macrina, Diane-Yayra Adjavon, Jan Funke, John C. Tuthill, Anthony Azevedo, and Benjamin L. de Bivort.

Funding was provided by the National Institutes of Health (grants R01NS121874; RF1MH117808; U19NS118246; U24NS126935; RF1MH117815; K99NS129759; R00NS117657; R01NS102333; RF1NS128785; R01NS140174; UM1NS132253; U24NS13992; RF1MH128840; R01NS121911; T32GM144273; R01DK139131; R25NS080687), a Sir Henry Wellcome Postdoctoral Fellowship (222782/Z/21/Z), a Smith Family Foundation Odyssey Award, a Harvard/MIT Joint Research Grant, an HHMI Life Sciences Research Foundation Postdoctoral Fellowship (PJ100000343), a New York Stem Cell Foundation Robertson Neuroscience Investigator Award, the Deutsche Forschungsgemeinschaft (ZA1296/1-1; EXC2151-390873048; PA787/7-3; PA787/9-3), the Nevada IDeA Network of Biomedical Research Excellence (GM103440), the National Science Foundation (2127379; 2014862), the Japan Society for the Promotion of Science (KAKENHI 25K00370), the Japan Science and Technology Agency (ASPIRE JPMJAP2302; CRONOS JPMJCS24K2), an HHMI Gilliam Fellowship (GT15790), the Max Planck Society, the Shanahan Family Foundation, a Kempner Graduate Fellowship, the Medical Research Council (MC_EX_MR/T046279/1), the Alice and Joseph Brooks Fund, and the Beijing Natural Science Foundation (IS23084). The authors also acknowledge that the work benefited from the O2 High-Performance Compute Cluster, supported by the Research Computing Group at HMS.

Harvard University filed a patent application for GridTape (WO2017184621A1) on behalf of the inventors, including W. Lee, and negotiated licensing agreements with interested partners. Macrina, Popovych, Kemnitz, Ih, K. Lee, Lu, Halageri, Bae, and Seung declare financial interest in Zetta AI. Seung declares financial interest in Memazing, Inc. Capdevila, Roberts, Langley, Munnelly, Griggs, and Moya-Llamas declare financial interest in Aelysia Ltd. Perlman is a principal of Yikes LLC.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a major scientific breakthrough in neuroscience with the complete mapping of the neural connections in a fruit fly's central nervous system. This achievement provides a comprehensive resource that advances understanding of brain-body coordination and motor control, with broad implications for neuroscience, AI, and potentially other species including humans. The open availability of the connectome and the detailed scientific context demonstrate significant substance and wide impact.
articles.personality_title: Scientists create the first complete map of a fruit fly’s nervous system
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists want to understand how the brain and body work together to control behavior. Fruit flies are often used in research because their nervous system is simpler than humans but still controls complex actions like walking and flying.

**What happened** – An international team led by Harvard Medical School and Princeton University mapped every neural connection in an adult fruit fly’s central nervous system. This map, called a connectome, includes the brain and the nerve cord (similar to a spinal cord). They used thousands of tiny slices of a fly and special microscopes to capture detailed images, then used computer tools to build a 3D map of all the neurons and their connections.

**Impact** – The map shows that control of movement in fruit flies is mostly handled by local circuits in each body part, not by one central brain area. For example, each leg’s movements are controlled by neurons near that leg, which then connect to other legs for coordinated walking. This challenges the old idea of a single brain controller and helps scientists understand how complex behaviors happen. The map is now free online, so researchers everywhere can use it to study brains and behavior.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to add more details to the map, like how neurons use special molecules to communicate. They also want to study if other animals, like mice, use the same local control system. This work could help scientists learn about human brains and improve artificial intelligence by showing how real nervous systems organize movement and behavior.

**One-sentence takeaway** – For the first time, scientists have mapped every neuron in a fruit fly’s brain and nerve cord, revealing that movement is controlled by many local circuits working together rather than a single brain center.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques créent la première carte complète du système nerveux d’une mouche des fruits
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cherchent à comprendre comment le cerveau et le corps travaillent ensemble pour contrôler le comportement. Les mouches des fruits sont souvent utilisées en recherche car leur système nerveux est plus simple que celui des humains tout en contrôlant des actions complexes comme marcher et voler.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe internationale dirigée par la Harvard Medical School et l’Université de Princeton a cartographié chaque connexion neuronale dans le système nerveux central d’une mouche des fruits adulte. Cette carte, appelée connectome, inclut le cerveau et la corde nerveuse (semblable à la moelle épinière). Ils ont utilisé des milliers de tranches très fines d’une mouche et des microscopes spéciaux pour capturer des images détaillées, puis des outils informatiques pour construire une carte 3D de tous les neurones et leurs connexions.

**Impact** – La carte montre que le contrôle des mouvements chez la mouche est surtout assuré par des circuits locaux dans chaque partie du corps, pas par une zone centrale unique dans le cerveau. Par exemple, les mouvements de chaque patte sont contrôlés par des neurones proches de cette patte, qui se connectent ensuite aux autres pattes pour marcher en coordination. Cela remet en question l’ancienne idée d’un contrôleur unique dans le cerveau et aide les scientifiques à comprendre comment des comportements complexes se produisent. La carte est maintenant disponible gratuitement en ligne pour que les chercheurs du monde entier puissent l’utiliser.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient d’ajouter plus de détails à la carte, comme la façon dont les neurones utilisent des molécules spéciales pour communiquer. Ils veulent aussi étudier si d’autres animaux, comme les souris, utilisent le même système de contrôle local. Ce travail pourrait aider à mieux comprendre le cerveau humain et à améliorer l’intelligence artificielle en montrant comment les vrais systèmes nerveux organisent les mouvements et le comportement.

**Phrase clé** – Pour la première fois, les scientifiques ont cartographié chaque neurone dans le cerveau et la corde nerveuse d’une mouche des fruits, révélant que le mouvement est contrôlé par plusieurs circuits locaux qui travaillent ensemble plutôt que par un centre cérébral unique.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos crean el primer mapa completo del sistema nervioso de una mosca de la fruta
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos quieren entender cómo el cerebro y el cuerpo trabajan juntos para controlar el comportamiento. Las moscas de la fruta se usan mucho en investigaciones porque su sistema nervioso es más simple que el de los humanos pero aún controla acciones complejas como caminar y volar.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo internacional liderado por la Escuela de Medicina de Harvard y la Universidad de Princeton mapeó cada conexión neuronal en el sistema nervioso central de una mosca de la fruta adulta. Este mapa, llamado conectoma, incluye el cerebro y la médula nerviosa (similar a la médula espinal). Usaron miles de cortes muy finos de una mosca y microscopios especiales para capturar imágenes detalladas, luego usaron computadoras para crear un mapa 3D de todas las neuronas y sus conexiones.

**Impacto** – El mapa muestra que el control del movimiento en las moscas es principalmente local en cada parte del cuerpo, no en una zona central del cerebro. Por ejemplo, el movimiento de cada pata está controlado por neuronas cerca de esa pata, que luego se conectan con otras patas para caminar coordinadamente. Esto desafía la idea antigua de un único controlador cerebral y ayuda a los científicos a entender cómo ocurren comportamientos complejos. El mapa ahora está disponible gratis en línea para que investigadores en todo el mundo lo usen.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean añadir más detalles al mapa, como cómo las neuronas usan moléculas especiales para comunicarse. También quieren estudiar si otros animales, como ratones, usan el mismo sistema de control local. Este trabajo podría ayudar a entender mejor el cerebro humano y mejorar la inteligencia artificial mostrando cómo los sistemas nerviosos reales organizan el movimiento y el comportamiento.

**Frase clave** – Por primera vez, los científicos han mapeado cada neurona en el cerebro y la médula nerviosa de una mosca de la fruta, revelando que el movimiento está controlado por muchos circuitos locales que trabajan juntos en lugar de un centro cerebral único.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a translucent adult fruit fly with its entire nervous system illuminated in soft, glowing lines, showing intricate neural connections spanning from its brain through the nerve cord to its legs and wings, symbolizing the complete neural map and distributed motor control within the fly’s body.

articles.title: Scientists propose a radical new theory for how life began on Earth
articles.summary: Researchers propose that tiny mineral nanoparticles may have been the hidden engines that transformed Earth’s early chemistry into the first building blocks of life. By acting as natural catalysts and energy processors, these “nanozymes” could help explain how lifeless matter gradually became living systems.
articles.content:
One of the biggest questions in science is how life first emerged on Earth. Researchers generally agree that the appearance of the first biopolymers and their building blocks marked a critical step in the origin of life (OoL). However, scientists still do not know exactly how a collection of prehistoric inert chemicals (gases) transformed into the first living systems.

The mystery remains difficult to solve because the full sequence of events that led to life is impossible to observe directly and extremely challenging to recreate. Over the past century, scientists have proposed numerous hypotheses, most of them centered on chemical evolution occurring either on Earth or in space. Yet each explanation has limitations, often relying on specific experimental findings and/or theoretical assumptions.

Several well known models have attempted to explain the (terrestrial) chemical OoL, including the Metabolism-first world (FeS world), Zinc world, Thioester world, RNA world, and Lipid world. While each provides valuable insights, none offers a complete explanation of how life emerged from nonliving matter. No single theory has successfully integrated all aspects of the process into a unified and convincing scenario.

A New Framework Built Around Nanozymes

To address this challenge, Prof. Yongdong Jin of the School of Biomedical Engineering at Shenzhen University in China has proposed the "nanozymes hypothesis" for the OoL on Earth.

The hypothesis suggests that primitive natural mineral nanozymes (MN-zymes), along with later generations of organic small molecule hybridized nanozymes, played a central role in the emergence and evolution of life. According to this idea, these materials were especially important during the earliest stages of life's development, helping generate the first biologically relevant molecules from nonliving substances.

Under primitive Earth conditions, MN-zymes may have gradually converted prehistoric inert chemicals (gases) into increasingly complex molecules through a combination of chemical (and physical) processes. The author proposes that this transformation occurred primarily through a process described as "inorganic photosynthesis."

Multiple Roles in Early Chemical Evolution

The nanozymes hypothesis assigns several important functions to natural MN-zymes. These include (a) catalysis, (b) surface binding/confinement, (c) anti-UV irradiation, (d) (photo-)selection, and (e) energy flow management.

By carrying out these roles, MN-zymes may have influenced early chemical reactions using natural sources of energy such as light, heat, and electricity. The hypothesis further suggests that they helped convert energy into molecular information stored in molecules (and entities) that could be read, written, and duplicated. Such capabilities are considered essential prerequisites for the emergence of living systems.

Earth as a Giant Natural Laboratory

The hypothesis views Earth itself as capable of gradually producing an organic world from an initially all-inorganic environment under harsh primordial conditions, an idea broadly consistent with earlier abiogenesis concepts.

In this framework, Earth functioned as a natural "all-in-one" chemistry laboratory operating over immense periods of time. Natural pressure gradients and temperature gradients throughout the planet (from mantle to crust), particularly near active volcanos and geothermal hot springs, may have provided ideal conditions for high temperature/high pressure lava reactions and hydrothermal reactions.

These environments could have generated the earliest MN-zymes, including metals/noble metals, metal oxides, and sulfide NPs. Notably, similar approaches are widely used today in laboratories to synthesize artificial nanozymes.

Over billions of years, this primordial collection of MN-zymes may have slowly evolved, renewed itself, and become increasingly sophisticated. Some may even have become incorporated into living organisms. According to the hypothesis, this process contributed to mineral evolution and gradual environmental changes that improved conditions for the survival and development of prebiotic molecules and primitive life.

Abundant Mineral Nanoparticles on Earth

Mineral NPs are already widespread throughout Earth's natural environments. Every year, thousands of terragrams (Tg) (1 Tg = 1012 g) of these particles circulate through ecosystems. Some possess natural enzyme-like activity and are therefore classified as MN-zymes.

These materials are found in oceans, waters, the atmosphere, and soils, where they play important roles in environmental biogeochemical cycles.

Recent discoveries also suggest that nature may produce MN-zymes more easily than previously thought. Studies have shown that NMs can form spontaneously through the weathering of natural minerals in charged water microdroplets or under UV irradiation. Sunlight and lightning may further provide the photocatalytic and electrocatalytic conditions needed to support the large scale production of both primordial nanozymes and later organic hybrid nanozymes, along with a rich supply of prebiotic molecules on Earth's surface.

The Proposed "Au World"

A particularly notable aspect of the hypothesis involves monolayer-protected gold NPs (AuNPs).

The author argues that these particles may have been among the most effective MN-zymes and could have occupied a central place in the evolutionary history of nanozymes during the OoL on Earth. He refers to this concept as the "Au world."

Although AuNPs are commonly regarded as artificial nanozymes today, the hypothesis suggests they were geologically plausible under a variety of natural Earth conditions.

Free AuNPs may have struggled to remain stable in the original soup because they generally require organic surface coatings. However, once small molecules such as thiols and amines were produced (by other MN-zymes) and accumulated in certain locations, AuNPs may have persisted in (thiols/amines) monolayer-protected forms. In this way, they could have participated in the broader network of reactions that contributed to life's emergence.

Four Key Conditions for Life Molecules

To further explain how life molecules may have been naturally selected and stabilized, the author identifies 4 essential elements and conditions related to the OoL on Earth:

Wet-dry cycling and amphiphilism

Self-assembly and self-organization

Catalytic and protoenzyme activity

Pairing symbiosis and stabilization

Together, these factors are proposed as fundamental requirements for the survival and evolution of early life related molecules.

Looking Ahead

The review extends beyond nanozymes themselves and explores several other major questions related to the OoL on Earth. These include the water paradox, the importance of the micro-nano structure of Earth's surface, and the unique physicochemical properties of water and dry-wet cycling environments that may have influenced prebiotic chemistry.

The author also discusses molecular cooperation and co-evolution during the earliest stages of life's emergence, as well as additional physical perspectives on the OoL, including ideas related to the chiral origin of biomolecules.

Ultimately, the nanozymes hypothesis is intended to provide a broader framework that may help reconcile long standing disagreements among competing origin-of-life theories. The author hopes it will shed new light on one of science's most enduring mysteries while also encouraging further research into the possible role of nanozymes in the emergence of life on Earth.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific hypothesis proposing a new framework for understanding the origin of life on Earth, which has broad implications for biology, chemistry, and the study of life's beginnings. It offers a detailed, substantive explanation of how mineral nanozymes could have catalyzed the transition from nonliving to living matter, addressing a fundamental and inspiring scientific mystery with potential to advance research and knowledge.
articles.personality_title: Scientists propose mineral nanoparticles helped start life on Earth
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long wondered how life first began on Earth. While many theories exist, none fully explain how nonliving chemicals turned into living things. This process, called the origin of life, remains a big mystery because it’s hard to study directly.

**What happened** – Professor Yongdong Jin from Shenzhen University in China suggested a new idea called the "nanozymes hypothesis." He thinks tiny particles made of minerals, called mineral nanozymes, acted like natural helpers that sped up chemical reactions. These particles could have turned simple chemicals into the first building blocks of life by using energy from sunlight, heat, and electricity on early Earth.

**Impact** – This theory is important because it offers a clear way to connect many parts of the origin of life puzzle. It explains how minerals might have worked like natural machines to create complex molecules needed for life. For example, gold nanoparticles protected by small molecules may have played a special role. The idea also fits with what we know about Earth’s early environments, like volcanic areas and hot springs, where these tiny particles could form and work.

**What's next step** – Researchers will likely conduct experiments to test how mineral nanozymes behave under conditions similar to early Earth. They may also study how these particles interact with other molecules and energy sources. This work could help scientists better understand the path from chemistry to biology and guide future studies on the origin of life.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Tiny mineral particles called nanozymes may have acted as natural catalysts that helped turn early Earth’s simple chemicals into the first living molecules.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques proposent que des nanoparticules minérales ont aidé à démarrer la vie sur Terre
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques se demandent depuis longtemps comment la vie a commencé sur Terre. Bien que plusieurs théories existent, aucune n’explique complètement comment des substances non vivantes sont devenues vivantes. Ce mystère, appelé l’origine de la vie, est difficile à étudier directement.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le professeur Yongdong Jin de l’Université de Shenzhen en Chine a proposé une nouvelle idée appelée « hypothèse des nanozymes ». Il pense que de toutes petites particules minérales, appelées nanozymes minérales, ont agi comme des aides naturelles accélérant les réactions chimiques. Ces particules auraient transformé des substances simples en premiers éléments de la vie en utilisant l’énergie du soleil, de la chaleur et de l’électricité sur la Terre primitive.

**Impact** – Cette théorie est importante car elle relie clairement plusieurs parties du puzzle de l’origine de la vie. Elle explique comment les minéraux auraient pu fonctionner comme des machines naturelles pour créer des molécules complexes nécessaires à la vie. Par exemple, des nanoparticules d’or protégées par de petites molécules pourraient avoir joué un rôle spécial. L’idée correspond aussi aux environnements connus de la Terre ancienne, comme les volcans et les sources chaudes, où ces particules pouvaient se former.

**Prochaines étapes** – Les chercheurs vont probablement faire des expériences pour tester le comportement des nanozymes minérales dans des conditions proches de celles de la Terre ancienne. Ils étudieront aussi comment ces particules interagissent avec d’autres molécules et sources d’énergie. Ces recherches pourraient aider à mieux comprendre la transition de la chimie à la biologie.

**Résumé en une phrase** – De minuscules particules minérales appelées nanozymes pourraient avoir agi comme des catalyseurs naturels qui ont aidé à transformer les substances simples de la Terre ancienne en premières molécules vivantes.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos proponen que nanopartículas minerales ayudaron a iniciar la vida en la Tierra
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han preguntado durante mucho tiempo cómo comenzó la vida en la Tierra. Aunque existen varias teorías, ninguna explica completamente cómo las sustancias no vivas se convirtieron en vivas. Este misterio, llamado origen de la vida, es difícil de estudiar directamente.

**Qué pasó** – El profesor Yongdong Jin de la Universidad de Shenzhen en China propuso una nueva idea llamada "hipótesis de las nanoenzimas". Él piensa que pequeñas partículas minerales, llamadas nanoenzimas minerales, actuaron como ayudantes naturales que aceleraron las reacciones químicas. Estas partículas podrían haber transformado sustancias simples en los primeros bloques de construcción de la vida usando energía del sol, calor y electricidad en la Tierra primitiva.

**Impacto** – Esta teoría es importante porque conecta claramente muchas partes del rompecabezas del origen de la vida. Explica cómo los minerales pudieron funcionar como máquinas naturales para crear moléculas complejas necesarias para la vida. Por ejemplo, nanopartículas de oro protegidas por pequeñas moléculas podrían haber tenido un papel especial. La idea también encaja con lo que se sabe sobre los ambientes tempranos de la Tierra, como volcanes y fuentes termales, donde estas partículas podrían formarse y actuar.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores probablemente harán experimentos para probar cómo se comportan las nanoenzimas minerales bajo condiciones similares a las de la Tierra temprana. También estudiarán cómo estas partículas interactúan con otras moléculas y fuentes de energía. Este trabajo puede ayudar a entender mejor el camino de la química a la biología.

**Resumen en una frase** – Pequeñas partículas minerales llamadas nanoenzimas podrían haber actuado como catalizadores naturales que ayudaron a convertir los químicos simples de la Tierra temprana en las primeras moléculas vivas.
articles.image_prompt: A warm-toned, detailed painting of a primordial Earth landscape featuring glowing mineral nanoparticles and delicate golden nanozyme clusters resembling tiny abstract molecular sculptures emerging from steaming volcanic hot springs and gently rippling wet-dry shorelines, symbolizing the natural catalytic processes that sparked the origin of life.

articles.title: Online tool that flags suspicious journals could warn researchers before submission
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 11 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01707-1The free platform, called Journal Trends, could also allow integrity sleuths to spot low-quality publications.
articles.content:
Journal Trends allows users to track a journal's published papers by country and year.Credit: Michael Szebor/Nature

Deciding where to publish just got a bit easier. An online tool that tracks publishing patterns in academic journals could warn researchers about potentially problematic journals before they submit their work to them, says the tool’s developer.

The platform, called Journal Trends, was released last month by its developer, Achal Agrawal, a data scientist and research-integrity sleuth based in India.

The growing number of journals makes it increasingly difficult for researchers to assess which publications are trustworthy, says research-integrity sleuth René Aquarius, a neurosurgery researcher at Radboud University Medical Center in Nijmegen, the Netherlands.

Agrawal says he used to spend hours manually scanning journals for signs that they were publishing a glut of low-quality studies. A common red flag is a sudden surge in the number of papers a journal publishes, particularly from authors in a single country, Agrawal says. This suggests that the journal is prioritizing publishing lots of papers without conducting proper peer review, he says. But spotting such patterns required painstaking analysis of publication data, journal by journal, says Agrawal, the founder of India Research Watch, an online group of researchers and students who highlight integrity issues.

So he built Journal Trends. The tool allows users to input a journal’s unique identification number and get a breakdown of published papers by country and year, he says.

The tool will also be useful for research-integrity investigators trying to determine whether suspicious papers reflect broader systemic issues in a journal, Aquarius says.

The data behind the tool come from OpenAlex, an open-source index that catalogues hundreds of millions of scientific documents, providing an alternative to commercial databases such as Scopus, which is owned by Dutch publishing company Elsevier.

Journal Trends also integrates data from the Problematic Paper Screener (PPS), a tool created by Guillaume Cabanac, a computer scientist at the University of Toulouse in France, and his colleagues, which scans millions of research papers every week for indicators of potential misconduct. Agrawal's tools can now generate shareable visualizations highlighting spikes in papers flagged by the PPS or unusual publication trends.

Slow delisting process

For example, data visualized through Journal Trends show that the International Journal of Advances in Signal and Image Sciences, which is published by XLE Science in India, published only 19 papers in 2024. But in 2025, publications jumped to 153, with 53% including an author in India.

Sudden publication surges alone do not prove a journal is untrustworthy, says Agrawal, but might indicate that researchers should investigate a journal further before submitting their work or paying article processing charges.

The journal was delisted from Scopus in 2025, meaning that it no longer appears in lists of trusted publications and is no longer counted for certain citation metrics. However, on its website, the journal continues to advertise its Scopus indexing. It charges up to US$1200 to process articles, and as of 28 May it had published 465 papers this year.
articles.reasoning: The article describes the development of an innovative online tool, Journal Trends, that helps researchers identify potentially low-quality or suspicious academic journals before submitting their work. This tool promotes research integrity by enabling early detection of problematic publishing patterns, benefiting the broader scientific community and improving the quality and trustworthiness of academic publishing.
articles.personality_title: New online tool helps researchers spot suspicious journals before submitting papers
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Researchers face a growing challenge in choosing trustworthy academic journals to publish their work because many journals vary in quality. Some journals publish too many low-quality papers, making it hard to know which ones are reliable.

**What happened** – A data scientist named Achal Agrawal created a free online tool called Journal Trends. Released in May 2026, it lets users check a journal’s publishing patterns by country and year. The tool shows if a journal suddenly publishes many more papers, especially from one country, which can be a warning sign of poor quality or weak review processes.

**Impact** – Journal Trends helps researchers avoid submitting to questionable journals by revealing unusual publication spikes. It also assists research investigators in spotting broader problems in journals. The tool uses data from OpenAlex, a large open-source database, and connects with another tool that flags possible misconduct in papers. This makes it easier to spot and understand risks in academic publishing.

**What's next step** – Researchers and institutions can start using Journal Trends to check journals before submitting papers. Over time, this could encourage journals to maintain higher standards. The tool may also help speed up the removal of unreliable journals from trusted lists, improving the quality of scientific publishing.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Journal Trends is a new free tool that helps researchers identify suspicious journals by tracking unusual publishing patterns, promoting better research quality and integrity.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouvel outil en ligne aide les chercheurs à repérer les revues suspectes avant soumission
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les chercheurs ont de plus en plus de mal à choisir des revues académiques fiables, car certaines publient beaucoup d’articles de faible qualité.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Achal Agrawal, un data scientist, a créé un outil en ligne gratuit appelé Journal Trends. Lancé en mai 2026, il permet aux utilisateurs de voir les tendances de publication d’une revue selon le pays et l’année. Si une revue publie soudainement beaucoup plus d’articles, surtout d’un seul pays, cela peut être un signe d’alerte.

**Impact** – Journal Trends aide les chercheurs à éviter les revues douteuses en montrant ces pics inhabituels de publications. Il aide aussi les enquêteurs à détecter des problèmes plus larges. L’outil utilise des données d’OpenAlex, une grande base ouverte, et se connecte à un autre outil qui repère les possibles fraudes dans les articles.

**Prochaines étapes** – Les chercheurs et institutions peuvent commencer à utiliser Journal Trends pour vérifier les revues avant de soumettre leurs articles. Cela pourrait pousser les revues à améliorer leurs standards et accélérer la suppression des revues peu fiables.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Journal Trends est un nouvel outil gratuit qui aide à identifier les revues suspectes grâce à l’analyse des tendances de publication, pour améliorer la qualité et l’intégrité de la recherche.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva herramienta en línea ayuda a investigadores a detectar revistas sospechosas antes de enviar artículos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los investigadores enfrentan cada vez más dificultades para elegir revistas académicas confiables, porque algunas publican muchos artículos de baja calidad.

**Qué pasó** – Achal Agrawal, un científico de datos, creó una herramienta en línea gratuita llamada Journal Trends. Lanzada en mayo de 2026, permite a los usuarios ver los patrones de publicación de una revista según el país y el año. Un aumento repentino en la cantidad de artículos, especialmente de un solo país, puede ser una señal de alerta.

**Impacto** – Journal Trends ayuda a los investigadores a evitar revistas dudosas al mostrar estos picos inusuales en las publicaciones. También ayuda a los investigadores de integridad a detectar problemas más amplios. La herramienta usa datos de OpenAlex, una gran base de datos abierta, y se conecta con otra herramienta que detecta posibles fraudes en los artículos.

**Próximos pasos** – Investigadores e instituciones pueden comenzar a usar Journal Trends para revisar las revistas antes de enviar sus trabajos. Esto podría motivar a las revistas a mejorar sus estándares y acelerar la eliminación de las no confiables.

**Frase resumen** – Journal Trends es una nueva herramienta gratuita que ayuda a identificar revistas sospechosas analizando patrones de publicación, promoviendo mejor calidad e integridad en la investigación.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a large, open book transforming into a network of glowing data points and country-shaped clusters, with one cluster noticeably growing larger and brighter to symbolize a surge, while a watchful magnifying glass made of light hovers protectively above, representing careful scrutiny and transparency in academic publishing.

articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant advancement in blue perovskite LEDs achieving high external quantum efficiency (21.8%) through a novel in situ polymerization-driven nanocrystal confinement technique. This breakthrough in LED technology has broad implications for energy-efficient lighting and display technologies, which can benefit society by reducing energy consumption and enabling better electronic devices. The article is focused on a single, well-defined technological innovation with detailed scientific context and references, indicating substance and significance.
articles.personality_title: New method boosts efficiency of blue perovskite LEDs to 21.8%
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Blue light-emitting diodes (LEDs) are important for screens and lighting but have been harder to make efficient than red or green LEDs. Perovskites are a type of material that can produce bright light but controlling their tiny crystals is challenging.

**What happened** – Scientists developed a new way to trap tiny perovskite crystals inside a polymer during their formation. This method, called in situ polymerization-driven nanocrystal confinement, was used to make blue perovskite LEDs that reached an external quantum efficiency (EQE) of 21.8%. EQE measures how well LEDs convert electricity into light.

**Impact** – Achieving over 20% EQE for blue perovskite LEDs is a big step because blue LEDs have been less efficient and less stable than other colors. This new approach helps the crystals stay uniform and stable, leading to brighter and longer-lasting blue LEDs. This could improve the quality and energy use of screens and lighting.

**What's next step** – Researchers will likely work on making these LEDs even more stable and easier to produce on a large scale. They may also explore how this method can be used for other colors or in new electronic devices.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new crystal-trapping technique has made blue perovskite LEDs more efficient, opening the door to better and greener lighting and display technologies.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle méthode améliore l'efficacité des LED au pérovskite bleues à 21,8 %
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les LED bleues sont essentielles pour les écrans et l'éclairage, mais elles sont plus difficiles à rendre efficaces que les LED rouges ou vertes. Les pérovskites sont des matériaux capables d'émettre une lumière intense, mais contrôler leurs nanocristaux est compliqué.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des scientifiques ont développé une méthode pour enfermer les nanocristaux de pérovskite dans un polymère pendant leur formation. Cette technique, appelée confinement des nanocristaux par polymérisation in situ, a permis de fabriquer des LED pérovskites bleues avec une efficacité quantique externe (EQE) de 21,8 %. L'EQE mesure la capacité des LED à transformer l'électricité en lumière.

**Impact** – Obtenir plus de 20 % d'EQE pour des LED pérovskites bleues est une avancée majeure car ces LED étaient moins efficaces et stables que les autres couleurs. Cette nouvelle méthode maintient les cristaux uniformes et stables, ce qui rend les LED plus lumineuses et durables. Cela pourrait améliorer la qualité et la consommation d'énergie des écrans et de l'éclairage.

**Étape suivante** – Les chercheurs vont probablement chercher à rendre ces LED encore plus stables et faciles à produire en grande quantité. Ils pourraient aussi tester cette méthode pour d'autres couleurs ou de nouveaux appareils électroniques.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une nouvelle technique de confinement des cristaux rend les LED pérovskites bleues plus efficaces, ouvrant la voie à un éclairage et des écrans meilleurs et plus économes en énergie.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo método aumenta la eficiencia de los LED de perovskita azul a 21,8 %
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los LED azules son importantes para pantallas y iluminación, pero han sido más difíciles de hacer eficientes que los LED rojos o verdes. Las perovskitas son materiales que pueden emitir luz brillante, pero controlar sus nanocristales es complicado.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos desarrollaron una nueva forma de atrapar los nanocristales de perovskita dentro de un polímero mientras se forman. Este método, llamado confinamiento de nanocristales por polimerización in situ, se utilizó para crear LED de perovskita azul que alcanzaron una eficiencia cuántica externa (EQE) del 21,8 %. La EQE mide qué tan bien un LED convierte electricidad en luz.

**Impacto** – Lograr más del 20 % de EQE en LED de perovskita azul es un gran avance porque estos LED han sido menos eficientes y estables que los de otros colores. Este nuevo enfoque ayuda a que los cristales sean uniformes y estables, produciendo LED azules más brillantes y duraderos. Esto podría mejorar la calidad y el consumo de energía en pantallas e iluminación.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores probablemente trabajarán en hacer estos LED más estables y fáciles de fabricar a gran escala. También podrían explorar cómo usar este método para otros colores o en nuevos dispositivos electrónicos.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Una nueva técnica para atrapar cristales ha hecho que los LED de perovskita azul sean más eficientes, abriendo camino a tecnologías de iluminación y pantalla mejores y más ecológicas.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of glowing, interconnected blue nanocrystals gently confined within a delicate lattice framework, emitting soft radiant light that symbolizes efficient blue perovskite LEDs, rendered with natural blues, soft grays, and subtle warm highlights on a simple, abstract background.
